summary: Jeff Atwood explores why Mac users and PC users fundamentally talk past each other when debating computer purchases. He cites Ed Stroglio's analysis that PC users view computers as tools (screwdrivers) while Mac users view them as extensions of personal identity (fashion). This explains why spec-based arguments about price and performance fail to persuade Mac users—they're operating from entirely different value systems. The post captures an early example of the utilitarian vs. experiential divide in technology consumption.
key_points: PC users treat computers as tools judged purely on functionality; Mac users treat them as personal identity statements
Case modders and overclockers differ from Mac users because they take pride in what they've done to equipment, not mere ownership
Telling a Mac user that a Dell is cheaper is like telling a fashionista that Old Navy overalls last longer than designer jeans
The disconnect between the two camps is fundamentally about different value systems, not ignorance of specs
Understanding this divide explains why 'standard arguments for buying a PC fall on deaf ears'
money_quotes: For PCers, a computer is a tool, an animated screwdriver. You don't have an 'experience' with a screwdriver.
For Macsters, the object is an extension of themselves just as much as their clothing or interior decoration.
What such people are proud of is not mere ownership of the equipment, but what they've done to it to make it what it is.
Telling them that a Dell is cheaper and better is like telling them that Old Navy overalls are cheaper and last longer than Dolce & Gabbana jeans.
When you do that, what they hear is, 'Be a common pig like the rest of us' when the whole point of the purchase is to prove the opposite.
You're thinking screwdriver; they're thinking fashion outfit.
key_insight: The Mac vs PC debate is irresolvable because each side operates from incompatible value systems—utility versus identity expression—making their arguments mutually incomprehensible.
